text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum a few centimeters above the mesh plug location, raising a peritoneal flap using sharp, blunt, and electrocautery dissection techniques. The lipoma of the spermatic cord is visibly reduced.

The surgeon focuses on the mesh plug, separating it from the surrounding tissues near the deep inguinal ring. The hernia sac is further dissected and completely reduced, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues dissection around the right Cooper's ligament within the space of Retzius, carefully reducing a small lipoma from the femoral canal.
The surgeon carefully dissects the mesh plug from the surrounding soft tissues, taking great care to avoid injury to the nearby testicular vessels and vas deferens.
The surgical field displays the testicular vessels, with the vessels positioned to the right and the vas deferens visible to the left, highlighting their close proximity.
Following the complete removal of the plug, the surgeon dissects the peritoneum proximally, creating a spacious area for the placement of the mesh.

The surgeon places a 10x15 cm self-gripping mesh in the preperitoneal space, centering it over the hernia defect. The mesh is carefully unrolled to ensure coverage of the deep inguinal ring, Hasselbach's triangle, and the femoral canal.

The surgeon achieves adequate positioning of the self-gripping mesh in the preperitoneal space and proceeds to close the peritoneal flap using a barb suture. A small hole in the peritoneum, previously adherent to the plug, is closed and reinforced with redundant hernia sac tissue to ensure separation from intra-abdominal organs.
